turns.content: okay so i've been deep in framer for the past few weeks and i think i'm in love? like genuinely this might be the most excited i've been about a tool in years
That's a strong reaction — what's got you so excited about it? Are you using it for prototyping, full design work, or something else?

everything honestly. i've fully moved over to it as my main tool. i'm doing all my UI work in it now, even starting branding explorations there. the interaction design capabilities are just on another level. like you can build real animations and transitions and the client actually sees them working in the prototype instead of just imagining what a static mockup would feel like in motion

That's a big shift — making it your primary tool for everything, not just a supplementary prototyping tool. The real-animation-in-prototype thing is genuinely powerful for client communication. So much design feedback goes wrong because clients can't mentally translate a static comp into a living interface.

How steep was the learning curve coming in? Did it click quickly or was there a rough adjustment period?

like the first week was rough, i'm not going to lie. the mental model is different because you're not just placing rectangles on a canvas, you're kind of building the actual thing. it thinks in components and interactions from the start, not as an afterthought. but once that clicked, honestly everything just made more sense. it's like going from sketching a building to actually constructing a scale model

That's a great analogy — the difference between representing something and actually building a working version of it. The fact that interactions and components are foundational rather than bolted on afterwards changes how you think about the design from the very beginning.

What specifically sold you? Was there a moment or a project where you thought "okay, this is it"?

yeah actually. i was working on this landing page for a client and i built the whole scroll animation system — like parallax layers, elements fading in on scroll, a header that transforms as you scroll down — and i could just send the client a link and they could see it all working live. no explaining, no "imagine this part moves when you scroll." they just saw it. and the feedback was so much more precise because they were reacting to the real thing

That's a huge workflow improvement. One of the biggest friction points in design is the translation layer between what you show and what gets built — and between what you show and what the client understands. If the prototype is essentially the real thing, both of those gaps shrink dramatically.

Are your clients noticing the difference in what you're delivering, or is it more of an internal workflow improvement for you?

oh they're noticing. like the landing page client literally said "wait this is already built?" and i had to explain that it was still a prototype but like... barely. you can actually publish directly from framer too so in some cases the prototype basically is the final product. that blew my mind honestly

The line between prototype and production getting that thin is genuinely significant for freelancers — it means you can potentially deliver more complete work without needing a separate development handoff for simpler sites. That changes the value proposition of what you're offering.

How's it handling the more detailed design work though? Typography control, color management, the precision stuff — does it hold up there or are there tradeoffs?

the typography controls are actually solid. not as granular as dedicated type tools obviously but for UI work it's more than enough. you get variable font support, good control over spacing and sizing, responsive text that actually reflows properly. the color management is fine too, it works in hex and rgb and hsl. for what i need it does the job. the only thing i miss a tiny bit is some of the vector editing for logo work but i still have illustrator for that

That sounds like a pretty manageable tradeoff — keep Illustrator for the heavy vector work like logos where you need full path editing control, and do everything else in Framer where the interaction capabilities justify the switch. Most designers maintain a couple of specialized tools alongside their primary environment anyway.

Have you been building up a component library in there yet, or is it still project-by-project?

already started a component library. that was actually one of the first things i did because i knew if i didn't build good foundations early i'd end up with a mess. i've got button systems, card layouts, nav patterns, all with built-in interaction states. hover, active, focus, the whole thing. and they're all responsive. honestly setting that up in framer felt way more natural than doing it anywhere else because the interactions are just part of the component definition, not a separate layer you bolt on

That's smart — building the library early with interactions baked in means every project from here benefits from that investment. And you're right that having interaction states as part of the component definition rather than a separate prototype layer is a fundamentally cleaner approach. It means your design system and your interaction model are the same thing.

Sounds like you've really committed to this. Do you see yourself going back to a more traditional static design tool, or is this the new baseline?

no going back honestly. like i tried opening my old static workflow yesterday to check something and it felt so flat. just dead rectangles on a screen. after working in framer where everything is alive and interactive, going back to static comps feels like going from color film to black and white. the whole paradigm just feels outdated to me now

That's usually the sign that a tool switch has really taken hold — when going back feels like a downgrade rather than a comfort zone. The fact that static mockups now feel limiting to you means your design thinking has already shifted to incorporate interaction as a core element, not an afterthought. That's a bigger change than just switching software.

Sounds like your clients are benefiting too, which is the part that actually matters for the business. Good move.

yeah exactly. and honestly from a freelance perspective being able to show working prototypes instead of flat mockups is a huge differentiator. like most designers in my market are still showing static screens and then there's me sending a link where everything moves and responds. it's been helping me win pitches which is the part that actually pays the bills lol

That competitive edge is the real payoff. In a freelance market where a lot of people offer similar services, being the one who shows a living prototype instead of a flat PDF is immediately differentiated. Clients don't have to imagine anything — they just experience it. That's worth the learning curve investment many times over.

Keep building that component library and keep winning pitches. Sounds like this was exactly the right call.
